modelInfo.name: Anthropic: Claude Opus 4.6
modelInfo.author: anthropic
modelInfo.description:
Opus 4.6 is Anthropic’s strongest model for coding and long-running professional tasks. It is built for agents that operate across entire workflows rather than single prompts, making it especially effective for large codebases, complex refactors, and multi-step debugging that unfolds over time. The model shows deeper contextual understanding, stronger problem decomposition, and greater reliability on hard engineering tasks than prior generations.

Beyond coding, Opus 4.6 excels at sustained knowledge work. It produces near-production-ready documents, plans, and analyses in a single pass, and maintains coherence across very long outputs and extended sessions. This makes it a strong default for tasks that require persistence, judgment, and follow-through, such as technical design, migration planning, and end-to-end project execution.

For users upgrading from earlier Opus versions, see our [official migration guide here](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/guides/model-migrations/claude-4-6-opus)

content.text:
So I've been working on this hacking themed minigame. See attached design doc.

I've got working prototypes of several of the core systems, and want to do a deep design dive before I start finalizing things.

Let's tackle these one at a time. I'll go over where the design sits currently and expansion angles I'm considering. We can go over things and flesh them out. Everything here is subject to change, so feel free to blue-sky.

First up, want to dig into the mini-puzzles, starting with the "Matrix search" now known as "Sniff."
______
Current implementation:
- Flexible-sized grid (4x4 minimum)
- In each grid cell is a single randomized hex value.
- Each column (or row) can scroll off the grid in either direction (up/down for columns, left/right for rows)
- scroll speed is modifiable, and different columns/rows can be scrolling in differebt directions or at different speeds
- The player is given one or more target hex value listed in a subwindow. They have to click the corresponding Hex values in the grid.
- if there are multiple targets, they can be clicked in any order. (Possibly, puzzles with multiple targets will require them to all be pressed within a window).

______

So that's where the design stands. Could use your help:
- analyzing the strengths and weaknesses of the current approach and possible solutions to problems
- fleshing out the existing mechanics
- pitching new "disruptors" as potential difficulty knobs or IC modules

Sniff is simple by design because it's meant to be quick to complete (~02 to ~10 seconds at most). So the biggest thing is just making sure it fits the fantasy and feels distracting without being annoying.

Feel free to use inspiration from real hacking or cyberpunk fiction as jumping off points.

# Sniff Minigame: Deep Design Dive

## Strengths & Weaknesses Analysis

### What's Working

- **Speed-appropriate complexity.** A visual search task in a shifting grid is almost pure attention cost, which is exactly what you want. It taxes the right resource (eyes-on-screen time) without demanding deep thought.
- **Naturally scalable.** Grid size, scroll speed, number of targets, and time window are all independent knobs you can tune without adding new rules.
- **Fits the fantasy.** Scanning scrolling hex data for a specific value is one of the most legible "hacking" visuals there is. It reads instantly.
- **Minimal onboarding.** "Find the thing, click the thing" is essentially zero-tutorial. Players understand it on contact.

### Potential Problems

| Problem | Why It Matters | Possible Mitigations |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual monotony** — a grid of same-format hex values can blur together, especially under pressure | If the player can't distinguish cells quickly, the puzzle becomes frustrating rather than tense. This is a "hard to perceive" problem, not a "hard to solve" problem, and those feel bad. | Vary cell styling subtly (font weight, slight color temperature shifts per column). Let targets have a distinct but not obvious visual tell once you know what to look for (see "Warm/Cold Pulsing" below). |
| **Pure reflex ceiling** — at high speeds, this becomes a reaction-time test, which alienates slower players and isn't really "hacking" | You want triage decisions, not twitch. If the scroll is too fast for a player to physically track, the puzzle stops being about attention-as-resource and starts being about raw APM. | Cap scroll speed at a human-readable rate and scale difficulty through *other* vectors instead (more targets, grid noise, IC disruptions). The grid should always be *readable*; the pressure should come from the opportunity cost of staring at it. |
| **Low decision density** — "find and click" has no meaningful choices once you've spotted the target | Every other system in your game is about triage and prioritization. Sniff currently has none. | Multi-target variants with a time window help. "Hex math" variants add a small decision layer. See expansion ideas below. |
| **Ambiguity between "hard to find" and "not currently on screen"** — if the target hex has scrolled off the grid, the player wastes time staring at nothing | This is the biggest feel-bad risk. Time spent searching for something that *literally isn't there* is dead time and it teaches the player not to trust the puzzle. | **Always guarantee the target is on-grid when the puzzle opens.** If scrolling moves it off, either wrap it around or provide a subtle edge indicator showing which direction the target is about to scroll in from. |

---

## Fleshing Out Core Mechanics

### Target Guarantees & Grid Behavior

This is worth nailing down explicitly:

- **On-open guarantee:** When a Sniff puzzle spawns, every target value exists somewhere on the visible grid. The player is never searching for something that isn't there *at the moment the window opens*.
- **Wrap vs. spawn:** When a cell scrolls off an edge, I'd recommend **wrapping** (it reappears on the opposite side of its row/column) rather than randomizing a new value. Wrapping means a player who *just* missed a target knows it's coming back around, which creates a satisfying "I'll catch it on the next pass" micro-decision. Random respawning means the target could simply vanish, which feels like the game cheated.
- **Duplicate values:** The grid should be allowed to contain duplicate hex values, including duplicates of the target. At low difficulty this won't happen often (small target list, large value space). At higher difficulty, intentional duplicates-of-non-targets act as visual noise. But the *target itself* should only appear once on the grid at any time to avoid confusion about whether you've already clicked the right one.

### Multi-Target Sequencing

You mentioned multi-target puzzles might require clicking within a window. I'd push this further into a design choice:

- **Unordered, no window (easy):** Click them in any order, no time coupling. Pure "find N things."
- **Unordered, shared window (medium):** Once you click the first target, a timer starts. All remaining targets must be clicked before it expires. This creates a natural tension: do you locate *all* targets visually before clicking the first one, or do you start clicking and hope you can find the rest in time?
- **Ordered sequence (hard):** Targets must be clicked in a specific order (displayed in the subwindow as a sequence). Now you might *see* the third target but can't click it yet, and it might scroll away before you get there. This is the highest-pressure variant without changing any base mechanics.

The "locate all before committing" decision in the shared-window variant is particularly nice because it mirrors the triage thinking of the main game. You're spending time now (scanning) to reduce risk later (not whiffing the window).

### Hex Math Variants

Your doc mentions this. Here's a concrete version:

> **Complement Sniff:** Instead of a literal target, the subwindow displays a target *sum* (e.g., `0xFF`) and one known operand (e.g., `0xB3`). The player has to find the cell containing `0x4C` (the complement). This is basic hex subtraction, which is really just "subtract each digit from F" for the `0xFF` case, making it learnable by pattern.

This is good because:
- It adds a tiny cognitive step (compute before searching) that makes the puzzle feel more like decryption.
- The `0xFF` complement case is elegant to learn: each hex digit just "mirrors" ($\text{F} - \text{B} = 4$, $\text{F} - 3 = \text{C}$). Players will internalize this fast.
- You can scale it by using sums other than `0xFF`, which breaks the simple mirror rule and requires actual mental math.

I'd introduce this as a variant that shows up after the player has done a few normal Sniffs, not as a replacement.

---

## Disruptors & Difficulty Knobs

### Difficulty Scaling Knobs (Non-IC, Baseline Tuning)

These are things you adjust based on mission difficulty, not tied to specific IC:

| Knob | Low | Medium | High |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grid size | 4×4 | 5×5 | 6×6+ |
| Target count | 1 | 2 | 3+ |
| Scroll speed | Slow, uniform | Moderate, varied | Fast, varied |
| Scroll direction variety | All same direction | Mixed directions | Per-column/row randomized |
| Sequencing requirement | Unordered | Shared window | Ordered |
| Value type | Literal match | Complement (`0xFF`) | Complement (arbitrary sum) |

### IC Disruptors

These are the fun ones. Each of these could be an IC module that a player might discover by scanning a Signal before hacking it, giving them a chance to prepare or choose to hack something else.

---

**IC: `PHANTOM`**
*Decoy targets appear on the grid.*

One or more cells visually pulse or highlight as if they're the target, but they're wrong values that are close to the real target (e.g., target is `0xE9`, phantom shows `0xE8` or `0xEB`). Clicking a phantom doesn't fail the puzzle but does something bad: small heat spike, brief screen flicker, or resets your multi-target progress.

*Why it works:* Punishes hasty clicking without punishing slow players. Encourages careful verification, which costs time — your real currency.

---

**IC: `BLACKOUT`**
*Grid cells intermittently go dark.*

Random cells (or entire rows/columns) temporarily display as `--` or go blank for 1–2 seconds at a time. The data is still "there" (scrolling continues, and if you happen to click a blacked-out cell that contains the target, it still counts), but you can't read it.

*Why it works:* Increases the scan time organically. Creates a rhythm of "wait for the cell to become readable" that interacts with scroll speed. Also sells the fantasy of fighting through electronic countermeasures.

---

**IC: `MIRROR`**
*Hex values are displayed in a transformed format.*

Values are shown bitwise-inverted, reversed, or in an alternate base (octal? binary nibbles?). The target in the subwindow is still shown normally. The player has to mentally translate.

Example: Target is `0xA7`. Grid shows values in binary-coded nibbles: `1010 0111`. Not hard to decode, but it takes a beat, and that beat matters when the grid is scrolling.

*Why it works:* Adds cognitive load without adding mechanical complexity. Fits the IC fantasy of "the system is fighting back against your intrusion." Players who've internalized hex-binary relationships are rewarded, but it's learnable.

---

**IC: `JITTER`**
*Scroll behavior becomes erratic.*

Instead of smooth, constant scrolling, columns/rows briefly stutter, pause, or lurch. The actual speed averaged over time doesn't change much, but the unpredictability makes visual tracking much harder.

*Why it works:* Breaks the rhythm the player has established. You can't just "feel" where a cell is going to be; you have to actively track it. Light touch, high impact.

---

**IC: `FLOOD`**
*Grid size temporarily expands.*

The grid adds an extra row and column (or two), and fills them with new random values. This happens mid-puzzle, after the player has already started scanning. The target is still in there, but the search space just got bigger and the layout shifted.

*Why it works:* Punishes tunnel vision. If you were about to click the target and the grid expands, your spatial memory is disrupted. Also naturally synergizes with the "grid size" difficulty knob.

---

**IC: `SPOOF`**
*The target value changes mid-puzzle.*

After a brief delay (enough time to have started searching for the original target), the subwindow updates with a new target value. Your previous visual search is wasted.

*Why it works:* This is *mean* — use sparingly and probably only at high heat or on high-value targets. It's a direct tax on time, which is your scarcest resource. The counterplay is scanning the Signal first to discover the IC and deciding whether to deal with it now or prioritize other threats. Very much in the spirit of triage-over-mastery.

---

### Meta-Disruptors (Interface-Level IC)

These cross the boundary between the Sniff window and the rest of the player's screen, fitting your Rhythm Doctor-inspired "meta-mechanics that affect the interface" pillar:

**`DRIFT`** — The Sniff popup window slowly moves across the screen, potentially obscuring the Signal Timeline. The player can drag it back, but that's a hand off the keyboard.

**`RESIZE`** — The popup window shrinks (or grows to obscure other elements). The grid stays the same logical size but becomes physically harder to read, or takes up too much screen real estate.

**`STATIC`** — Visual noise (scanlines, color distortion) is overlaid on the Sniff window specifically, simulating the target system fighting back against the intrusion. Doesn't change the puzzle, but increases eye strain and scan time.

---

## A Note on Feel

The biggest risk with Sniff across all of these variants is that it becomes a **chore**. It's a simple puzzle by design, which means it can't carry interest on its own — it has to derive its tension from *context*. The grid scrolling past while the Signal Timeline is also scrolling past, while heat is ticking up, while another camera is about 8 seconds from the runners — *that's* what makes Sniff feel good.

So I'd keep the baseline Sniff almost trivially easy (single target, slow scroll, small grid, ~2-3 seconds to solve) and let the *situation* provide most of the difficulty. IC disruptors should be visible on scan so the player can make informed triage decisions about them. The puzzle should never be the enemy; the *clock* should be.

---

Want to go deeper on any of these, or move to the next minigame?

I think that's about as much as we can get from Sniff, so let's look at Decrypt as it's the other locked-in and prototyped minigame.

Here's how it currently works:
- A variable-length (default 3 or 4 characters) cipher is at the top of the window.
- below it are corresponding input fields for the player to input the solution.
- below each of those, are a set of "key spaces" that list 3 or more possible characters that could be the answer for that slot
- every X seconds (currently 10), the decrypt program culls at least 1 possible answer
- the player can choose one keyspace to "focus" - while focused, twice as many wrong answers are culled from that key space
- the player can input an answer at any time.
- A wrong answer locks that input spot for X (default 2) seconds
- A correct answer gives a 5 second boost, during which the cull rate increases by X% (base 66%)
- when a keyspace collapses to the correct answer, it's automatically filled into the input slot
- all ciphers are based on relatively simple and human-crackable encryption types (my test case for the prototype is simply ABCD -> FGHI, but higher levels might involve more complex encryption and non-consecutive characters)

That's where it stands now. Hit me with the same analysis as we did with Sniff.

# Decrypt Minigame: Deep Design Dive

## Strengths & Weaknesses Analysis

### What's Working

- **Natural "process delay" cadence.** This is the puzzle type you called out in the doc as incentivizing the player to deal with other things while waiting. The 10-second cull timer is a built-in reason to tab away and come back. That's perfect for the plate-spinning fantasy.
- **Meaningful decision embedded in a simple frame.** "Which keyspace do I focus?" is a genuine micro-triage decision. Do you focus the slot you're most confused about, or the one that's closest to collapsing anyway to trigger the correct-answer cascade?
- **Risk/reward on early guessing.** The player can always try to brute-force an answer early, but the lockout punishment means a wrong guess at the wrong time could be costly. This creates a nice tension between patience and urgency that mirrors the main game's triage philosophy.
- **Cascading momentum.** The correct-answer boost accelerating other keyspaces is satisfying. It creates a tipping point where the puzzle "breaks open" and the last few slots collapse quickly, which feels like cracking a code should feel.
- **Scales naturally with the cipher.** Moving from ABCD→FGHI (constant offset) to more complex transforms gives you a difficulty curve that rewards pattern recognition without requiring the player to be a cryptographer.

### Potential Problems

| Problem | Why It Matters | Possible Mitigations |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive waiting feels dead.** The optimal play pattern might be: open Decrypt, set focus, tab away for 30 seconds, come back when it's mostly solved. If the puzzle plays itself, the player's contribution doesn't feel meaningful. | The puzzle needs to feel like you *cracked* it, not like you *waited* it out. The timer should be a safety net for players who can't deduce the cipher, not the default path for everyone. | Weight the design to reward deduction heavily (see "Deduction Payoff" below). Make passive completion slow enough that it's a real cost in heat/time compared to active solving. |
| **The cipher pattern is either obvious or irrelevant.** If the player spots the transform early ("+5 to each letter"), they can solve instantly and the whole keyspace mechanic is wasted. If they can't spot it, they ignore the cipher entirely and just wait for keyspaces to collapse. There's no middle ground. | You want a spectrum of engagement where partial deduction is useful, not a binary "got it / didn't get it" switch. | Make partial deduction mechanically useful. If a player figures out the rule for *one* slot, let that feel rewarding even if they can't generalize to all slots yet. See "Partial Deduction" below. |
| **Focus is a "set and forget" decision.** You pick a keyspace to focus, tab away, come back. There's no reason to change focus dynamically unless you're making a very specific optimization play. | A decision you make once and never revisit isn't very interesting. | Give the player reasons to shift focus mid-puzzle. Changing focus could have a small cost (brief pause in culling). Some IC could disrupt focus. See disruptors. |
| **Lockout on wrong answer is pure punishment with no texture.** 2 seconds of "you can't interact with this slot" is a time penalty, but it doesn't create any interesting gameplay. The player just waits. | Punishment should ideally create a new decision, not a dead moment. | See "Wrong Guess Consequences" redesign below. |
| **Scaling complexity of cipher types could be an onboarding nightmare.** If later missions use substitution ciphers, transpositions, or mixed transforms, the player needs to learn these *somewhere* and the middle of a tense run is the wrong place. | Players who feel like they *should* be able to crack the cipher but don't understand the transform type will feel dumb, not challenged. | Introduce new cipher types in low-pressure contexts first (maybe the management layer lets you "practice" decryption). Show the cipher *type* name somewhere in the puzzle window so players can learn to recognize them. |

---

## Fleshing Out Core Mechanics

### Deduction vs. Waiting: Getting the Balance Right

The central tension of Decrypt is: **do I try to crack the cipher myself, or do I let the system brute-force it while I handle other things?** Both paths should be viable, but deduction should be *significantly* faster and cheaper.

Here's a concrete framework:

**Passive solve time (no player input, just letting culls happen):**
- 3-character cipher: ~40-60 seconds
- 4-character cipher: ~60-90 seconds

**Active deduction solve time (player spots the pattern):**
- Instant for any slot where you've figured out the transform

**Hybrid path (partial deduction + waiting):**
- Somewhere in between, with the correct-answer boost accelerating the remaining passive solves

If the passive path takes 60+ seconds on a door that's 30 seconds away on the timeline, the player *has* to engage with the cipher to solve it in time. But if they're dealing with three other threats, they can open the Decrypt, set focus, and accept that it'll resolve slowly while they triage. Both are valid plays, and the choice between them is triage itself.

### Partial Deduction Mechanics

This is where I think the biggest design gains are. Right now, deduction is binary: you either see the full pattern or you don't. Here's a way to make partial insight mechanically rewarding:

**Manual Cull:** Let the player click on a specific wrong answer in a keyspace to eliminate it, *if they can justify it through the cipher pattern.* This doesn't need to literally require the player to explain their reasoning — just let them click to eliminate candidates manually at any time. If they're right (that candidate was indeed wrong), it's removed instantly. If they're wrong (they accidentally eliminated the correct answer), there's a consequence.

This means a player who notices "the offset seems to be around +4 to +6" can start eliminating candidates outside that range even if they haven't nailed down the exact transform. Partial knowledge becomes partial progress.

The wrong-elimination consequence should be meaningful but not catastrophic: maybe it freezes culling on that keyspace for one cycle, or adds a small heat bump. Something that discourages random guessing but doesn't destroy the player for one bad deduction.

### Wrong Guess Consequences (Redesigned)

Instead of a flat lockout, consider a **cascading penalty** that interacts with the rest of the puzzle:

- **First wrong guess on a slot:** That slot locks for 2 seconds (current behavior). Fine.
- **Second wrong guess on the same slot:** Lock extends to 5 seconds, AND culling pauses on *all* keyspaces for one cycle. The system is "recalibrating" after your failed attempts. Now over-guessing on one slot hurts your progress everywhere.
- **Third wrong guess on the same slot:** Small heat spike. The brute-force attempts are generating noise that corporate security can detect.

This turns wrong guesses from a flat time-out into a triage decision: "I'm pretty sure it's one of these two remaining candidates, but if I'm wrong, it'll cost me across the whole puzzle. Do I guess, or wait one more cull cycle?"

### Cipher Types as a Progression System

Rather than surprising the player with new cipher types mid-run, treat them like learnable skills:

| Tier | Cipher Type | Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Caesar (Constant Offset)** | Each character shifts by the same fixed value | A→F, B→G, C→H (+5) |
| 2 | **Stride (Variable Offset)** | Each character shifts by an incrementing value | A→C, B→E, C→H (+2, +3, +5: primes) |
| 3 | **Substitution** | Each character maps to a specific unrelated character | A→Q, B→D, C→M (lookup table) |
| 4 | **Transposition** | Characters are correct but reordered | ABCD → CDAB (swap halves) |
| 5 | **Mixed** | Combines two types | Caesar + Transposition |

The key insight: **the cipher type should be visible metadata, not a secret.** The puzzle window should say something like `CIPHER: OFFSET` or `CIPHER: SUBSTITUTION` so the player knows *what kind* of pattern to look for. The challenge is spotting the specific parameters, not identifying the category. This keeps the onboarding manageable and lets players develop expertise with specific types over time.

---

### The Focus Mechanic, Refined

Currently, focus doubles cull rate on one keyspace. Here are some ways to make it more dynamic:

**Focus Decay:** Focus effectiveness starts high and diminishes over time on the same keyspace. After ~15 seconds of continuous focus on one slot, you're getting diminishing returns. This naturally encourages the player to rotate focus, creating periodic "check-in" moments where they come back to the Decrypt window, assess the state, and shift focus.

**Focus as Magnifying Glass:** While a keyspace is focused, the player gets a subtle visual hint about the cipher — maybe the focused slot's cipher character pulses or highlights its relationship to nearby characters. This gives the observant player deductive information as a reward for engaging with focus actively rather than passively.

---

## Disruptors & Difficulty Knobs

### Baseline Scaling Knobs

| Knob | Low | Medium | High |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cipher length | 3 | 4 | 5–6 |
| Candidates per keyspace | 3 | 4–5 | 6+ |
| Cull interval | 8 sec | 10 sec | 14 sec |
| Cipher type | Constant offset | Variable offset / Substitution | Mixed |
| Correct-answer boost | +66% cull rate | +50% | +33% |

### IC Disruptors

---

**IC: `REKEY`**
*The cipher transform changes mid-puzzle.*

Once the player has partially solved the cipher, the system re-encrypts with a new key. Already-solved slots stay solved (the system can't un-reveal what you've already cracked), but all remaining keyspaces repopulate with new candidates based on the new transform. The cipher type stays the same — only the parameters change (e.g., offset shifts from +5 to +3).

*Why it works:* Punishes the "wait it out" strategy hard, since passive progress gets wiped. Players who were actively deducing can re-derive the new key quickly since they already understand the cipher type. Rewards engagement.

---

**IC: `NOISE`**
*False candidates are periodically injected into keyspaces.*

Every other cull cycle, instead of only removing wrong answers, the system also *adds* one new wrong candidate to a random unsolved keyspace. Net progress is still positive (removing more than adding), but it slows things down and makes the keyspaces feel unstable.

*Why it works:* Creates a low-grade anxiety. The player can't just set focus and leave for 30 seconds — they need to check back and maybe manually cull the obvious fakes. Pairs well with the "manual cull" mechanic proposed above. Doesn't hard-block the player, just taxes their attention.

---

**IC: `DEADLOCK`**
*Two keyspaces become linked. Culling one pauses the other.*

The system designates two unsolved slots as entangled. Whenever the player focuses one, culling on the other freezes entirely. Culling still happens passively on the unfocused one when neither is focused, but at a reduced rate.

*Why it works:* Turns focus into a genuine dilemma. You *can't* optimize both linked slots simultaneously. Do you brute-force one with focus and accept the other will be slow? Or do you try to deduce the linked pair and bypass the lock entirely? Creates a puzzle-within-the-puzzle.

---

**IC: `MASK`**
*Solved keyspaces don't auto-fill.*

Normally, when a keyspace collapses to one answer, it auto-fills the input slot. With MASK active, collapsed keyspaces just sit there with one candidate — the player still has to manually fill it in. This seems trivial, but it means the player has to *check back* after each collapse rather than trusting the puzzle to finalize itself.

*Why it works:* Light touch, high nuisance factor. Specifically punishes the "set and forget" pattern. The player might tab back to the Decrypt and find two keyspaces collapsed and waiting for input while a third just got its candidates refreshed by NOISE. Low-key chaos.

---

**IC: `SCRAMBLE`**
*Keyspace candidates are displayed in a randomized or obscured format.*

Instead of showing clean characters, candidates are displayed as hex codes, unicode references, or some other encoded format. The player has to mentally decode each candidate before they can reason about the cipher. For example, a keyspace might show `0x46 0x47 0x48` instead of `F G H`.

*Why it works:* Increases cognitive load on the deduction path specifically. Players who are just waiting for culls don't care what format the candidates are in — they're just waiting for one to remain. But players trying to spot the cipher pattern now have an extra translation step. This is mean but fair because the encoding is learnable (and players who've been doing Sniff hex math will have a head start).

---

### Meta-Disruptors

**`CORRUPT`** — One of the "solved" input slots occasionally glitches and appears to clear itself. It hasn't actually cleared (the answer is still locked in), but the visual flicker forces the player to check whether they need to re-enter it. Pure attention tax.

**`INTERCEPT`** — A second, fake Decrypt window opens alongside the real one, with a similar-looking but slightly different cipher. The player has to identify which window is the real puzzle. The fake one has a subtle tell (wrong cipher type label, different border color, slightly off formatting). Feeds directly into your Papers Please "subtle attention checks" pillar.

---

## Interaction with Terminal Layer

One thing worth calling out: you mentioned in the doc that the Terminal minigame might serve as a "core mini-mini game that interacts with other puzzle types." Decrypt is the natural place for that integration.

Some ideas:

- **Terminal pre-step:** Before the Decrypt window opens, the player has to run a terminal command to initiate the decryption process. Something like `DECRYPT <signal_id> -t offset`. If they identify the cipher type wrong in the command flag, the initial keyspaces are wider (more candidates). If they get it right, they start with fewer candidates. This rewards players who scanned the signal first and saw the cipher type.
- **Terminal-assisted cull:** Mid-puzzle, the player could switch to the terminal and run a command to force an immediate cull cycle on a specific slot. Something like `CULL -s 3` to cull slot 3. This costs heat (you're running extra decryption cycles) but gives the player an active lever to pull when they need to speed things up.
- **Terminal brute-force:** A costly terminal command that instantly solves one slot but generates a significant heat spike. The "break glass in case of emergency" option.

These give the Terminal layer a functional role in Decrypt beyond just opening the window, and they create small command-language decisions that feed the "internalize the system and feel clever" loop you described in the doc.

---

## Summary: Decrypt's Role in the Ecosystem

If Sniff is the **fast attention check** (2-10 seconds, demanding eyes-on-screen), Decrypt is the **slow background process** (30-90 seconds, demanding periodic check-ins and deductive reasoning). Together they create a nice rhythm: Sniff pulls you in hard and releases you fast; Decrypt asks you to keep spinning a plate over a longer window.

The biggest design priority should be making sure the deduction path is always more rewarding than the passive path, so that Decrypt feels like *cracking a code* and not *watching a progress bar*.

---

Want to dig into any of these threads further, or move on to the next system?

One more to dig into. This is one I just came up with and haven't prototyped or fleshed out.

I like the spaces of timing windows, probing weaknesses and "stacking small successes," inspired by real hackers paradigms like fuzzing.

Here's the idea:

- You have a circle with a smaller circle in the center as it's "core"

- moving the mouse let's you rotate a little "data cannon" along the inside edge of the outer circle (the barrel facing toward the core)

- every X seconds, you get X packets of data that you can shoot at the core, they take a few seconds to hit

- depending on how close to the randomly generated "sweet spot" you are, the packets... do more of the thing (damage the core, raise a progress bar, whatever victory state we decide)

- there's a visual indicator on hit showing how effective the hits are (so the sweet spots are clearly differentiated)

- goal is to quickly locate by probing around the circle and then blast damage into the sweet spot once it's located

# "Fuzz" Minigame: Design Exploration ## Initial Read The core loop here is **probe → locate → exploit**, which is a genuinely great hacking fantasy arc. That's literally the structure of real penetration testing: you scan for vulnerabilities, identify a weak point, then hammer it. The fact that the mechanic naturally produces that narrative without any flavor text is a strong sign. Let's break it down. ## Strengths - **Skill expression through deduction, not reflexes.** The player is making educated guesses, narrowing a search space, then capitalizing. That's a different cognitive muscle than Sniff (visual search) or Decrypt (pattern recognition), which gives you good variety across your minigame suite. - **Readable at a glance.** A circle with a thing you're pointing at the center is visually clean and immediately parseable even in a small popup window. You can tell what's happening from across the room. - **Natural escalation built into the structure.** Early shots are exploratory and low-value. Later shots are precise and high-value. The puzzle has a built-in dramatic arc every time you play it. - **"Ammo economy" creates micro-triage.** You get X packets every Y seconds. Do you spread them to probe, or concentrate them once you think you've found the spot? That's a real decision. ## Potential Problems & Design Questions ### The Big One: What Happens Between Volleys? You get packets every X seconds. The packets take a few seconds to travel. That means there are dead windows where you're just... watching dots float toward the center and waiting for feedback. During a 2-second travel time plus a multi-second reload, the player has nothing to do in this window. This is either a **problem** or a **feature**, depending on how you frame it: - **Problem framing:** Dead air in a puzzle that's supposed to require attention. The player twiddles their thumbs. - **Feature framing:** Built-in "process delay" cadence, just like Decrypt. The player fires a volley, tabs to the Signal Timeline to handle something else, tabs back when the next volley is ready. I think you want the **feature framing**, but it needs some design support to feel intentional. Specifically: - The feedback from hits should persist visually so the player can tab away and read the results when they come back (see "Heat Map" below). - The reload timer should be visible and predictable so the player knows exactly when to check back. - Travel time should be short enough that you can fire-and-read within one interaction window if you choose to stay, but long enough that tabbing away feels efficient. **Suggested timing:** ~1.5 second travel time, ~4-5 second reload. Fast enough that staying in the window doesn't feel wasteful, slow enough that tabbing out is rewarded. ### Sweet Spot Readability The player needs clear, instant feedback on "how close am I?" without the game just *telling* them where the sweet spot is. This is a signal-clarity problem. **Heat Map Residue:** Every packet that hits the core leaves a colored mark at the angular position it struck. Color maps to effectiveness: cold blue/grey for low, warm orange for medium, bright red/white for direct hit. These marks persist for the duration of the puzzle. Over time, the circle's inner ring accumulates a visual record of everywhere you've probed and how effective each shot was. This is great for several reasons: - The player builds a mental map through experimentation, which *is* the hacking fantasy. - When they tab back to the window after handling other business, they can instantly read the state of the puzzle from the residue pattern. - It looks cool. A ring of scattered cold-blue marks with a cluster of hot-red marks where you've zeroed in on the vulnerability is visually satisfying. **Gradient feedback, not binary.** Effectiveness should fall off smoothly from the sweet spot, not in hard tiers. If the sweet spot is at 90°, a shot at 85° should be noticeably better than one at 70°, which should be noticeably better than one at 180°. This gives the player continuous information with every shot. Hard tiers create ambiguity ("I'm in the orange zone but I don't know if I'm on the left edge or right edge of it"). ### Sweet Spot Size & Precision How wide is the sweet spot? This is your primary difficulty knob, but it also determines the feel of the puzzle. | Sweet Spot Arc | Feel | Probe Shots to Locate | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | ~60° (1/6 of circle) | Forgiving, fast. Player finds it in 1-2 volleys. | 2-3 | Low-difficulty signals, cameras | | ~30° (1/12 of circle) | Moderate. Requires a systematic sweep. | 4-6 | Standard difficulty, drones | | ~15° or less | Punishing. Requires precise narrowing. | 6-10+ | High-security targets, doors with heavy IC | I'd also consider having a **peak within the sweet spot** — the effectiveness isn't flat across the arc but has a bell-curve shape with a true center. This means even after finding the general sweet spot zone, there's value in fine-tuning your aim for maximum damage. Players who are precise get rewarded without making imprecise players fail entirely. ### "Damage the Core" — Victory Condition You mentioned this is undefined. Here are three models, each with a different feel:
Model A: Health Bar (Simple, Direct) The core has an HP pool. Effective hits drain it. Puzzle is "solved" when HP reaches zero. Simple, readable, satisfying. - **Pro:** Immediately legible. Player always knows how close they are to done. - **Pro:** Partial progress is inherently preserved — even inefficient probing shots chip away at health. - **Con:** Can feel grindy if HP is high and the player hasn't found the sweet spot yet. **Best for:** General-purpose use. Default model.
Model B: Threshold Breach (Burst Damage) The core has a shield that regenerates slowly. Hits reduce the shield; if the shield is fully depleted in a single volley (or short window), the core cracks and the puzzle is solved. Scattered probing shots get healed back. The player has to find the sweet spot AND deliver a concentrated burst. - **Pro:** Creates a clear two-phase structure: probe phase (finding the spot) → exploit phase (concentrated burst). Very satisfying crack moment. - **Pro:** Punishes unfocused play and rewards the probe→locate→exploit arc you described. - **Con:** Potentially frustrating if the player finds the sweet spot but can't muster enough burst damage in one volley to breach the threshold. **Best for:** Higher-difficulty or higher-stakes signals where the puzzle should feel more climactic.
Model C: Exposure Timer (Hold the Line) The sweet spot must receive sustained effective hits over a cumulative duration to "crack." A progress bar fills while you're landing effective hits and slowly drains when you're not. You don't need to keep it sustained in one go — it's cumulative but decaying. - **Pro:** Creates ongoing tension. The player has to keep coming back to this puzzle and landing good hits. - **Pro:** Natural synergy with the plate-spinning gameplay. "I've got the Fuzz 60% cracked, but I need to deal with this camera. The progress will decay a bit but I can pick it up when I get back." - **Con:** Can feel like a treadmill if the decay rate is too high. **Best for:** Longer engagements, maybe high-value optional objectives where the player is committing sustained attention over time.
My instinct is that **Model A is your default** and **Model B is your high-difficulty variant**. Model C is interesting but might be better as an IC modifier than a base model (see `REGEN` below). --- ## Expansion Mechanics ### Multiple Sweet Spots At higher difficulty, the core could have **2-3 sweet spots** of varying sizes. The player has to locate and exploit all of them to solve the puzzle (or they all contribute to a shared HP pool but at different effectiveness rates). This naturally extends the probe phase and rewards systematic sweeping. You could even make the sweet spots **shift slowly** — rotating around the circle at a crawl. The player has to track a moving target. At low rotation speeds this is barely noticeable; at higher speeds it demands continuous attention. ### Packet Types Instead of all packets being identical, the player could have a limited loadout of packet types: - **Probe packets (default, unlimited or high count):** Low damage, normal feedback. Your bread and butter for finding the sweet spot. - **Exploit packets (limited, earned or cooldown-based):** High damage, but you only get a few per reload cycle. These are what you save for when you've found the sweet spot. - **Scatter packets (limited):** Fire in a wide spread arc (like a shotgun). Low individual damage but cover a large angular range. Good for initial probing of unexplored areas. This adds a micro-resource decision to each volley: "Do I probe or exploit? Am I confident enough in this position to spend my limited heavy ammo?" ### Volley Aiming: Spread vs. Focus Rather than (or in addition to) packet types, let the player choose a **spread width** for each volley. Wide spread covers more arc but each packet hits with less concentrated force. Narrow spread concentrates on a small arc for maximum damage but wastes the whole volley if you're aimed wrong. This is elegant because it maps directly to the probe/exploit phases. Early: wide spread. Late: narrow focus. And the transition point — when do you stop probing and start exploiting — is a meaningful decision. --- ## IC Disruptors --- **IC: `ROTATE`** *The sweet spot moves.* The vulnerability slowly rotates around the circle. Speed can vary — at low intensity, it's a gentle drift the player can track; at high intensity, it's fast enough that the player has to lead their shots to account for travel time. *Why it works:* Transforms the puzzle from "find then exploit" into "track and exploit." The probe phase never truly ends because the target is always moving. Pairs brutally with long travel times. --- **IC: `SHELL`** *The core has an outer defense layer that absorbs hits.* A visible ring around the core periodically activates, blocking all incoming packets for 1-2 seconds. The player has to time their volleys to arrive during the gaps. The shell's cycle is predictable and readable (it visually pulses). *Why it works:* Adds a timing layer on top of the aiming layer. The player has to synchronize their volleys with the shell's cycle, which means they can't just fire on cooldown and tab away — they have to watch and time it. Creates a rhythm-game feel without being a rhythm game. --- **IC: `DEFLECT`** *Packets that miss the sweet spot ricochet and generate heat.* Normally, probing shots that hit ineffective areas are low-cost. With DEFLECT, every miss generates a small heat tick proportional to how far from the sweet spot it landed. Now probing isn't free — every exploratory volley has a cost. *Why it works:* Directly attacks the "probe freely, exploit later" loop. The player has to be more conservative and deliberate with their probing, or accept the heat cost of an aggressive search. Pairs with the triage philosophy — is it worth generating heat to find this vulnerability faster? --- **IC: `SPLINTER`** *The sweet spot fractures into 2-3 smaller sweet spots on first effective hit.* The player finds the vulnerability, fires an effective volley, and then the sweet spot shatters into multiple smaller zones spread around the circle. All of them need to be exploited to complete the puzzle. *Why it works:* Subverts the expected arc. The player thinks they're in the exploit phase and suddenly they're back in probe mode. It's the Fuzz equivalent of Decrypt's `REKEY`. Psychologically disruptive without being unfair since the player can see the new fragments and the original heat map residue still helps narrow the search. --- **IC: `MIRROR`** *A decoy sweet spot appears opposite the real one.* The core shows TWO apparent sweet spots, but only one is real. The decoy gives warm-colored feedback (orange/yellow) but never hot (red/white). A player paying close attention will notice the decoy's effectiveness plateaus, while the real one keeps climbing. A player in a hurry might waste several volleys on the decoy before realizing. *Why it works:* Rewards careful observation of the heat map residue. If both spots show orange but only one shows deep red at its center, the player can distinguish them. Punishes "good enough" play without being invisible. --- **IC: `REGEN`** *The core slowly regenerates health between volleys.* Damage dealt to the core decays over time when the core isn't being hit. The decay rate is slow enough that consistent effective hits will still win, but long gaps (from tabbing away to handle other signals) mean lost progress. *Why it works:* This is Model C from the victory condition discussion, weaponized as an IC. It specifically punishes the plate-spinning pattern by making the player commit more sustained attention to this puzzle. Creates a direct tension: "Do I stay on this Fuzz and finish it, or handle that camera that's about to hit the runners?" --- ### Meta-Disruptors **`INVERT`** — The cannon flips to the *outside* of the circle, firing inward. Same mechanic, but the visual orientation is reversed. The player's mouse movement maps differently to the cannon's position, briefly disrupting muscle memory. **`STATIC`** — Hit feedback becomes unreliable. Some percentage of hits show randomized effectiveness colors instead of accurate ones. The player has to fire multiple volleys at the same position to get a reliable read, increasing the time cost of probing. The heat map becomes noisy and harder to interpret. --- ## Where Fuzz Fits in the Ecosystem You now have three minigames with distinct cognitive profiles: | Minigame | Duration | Attention Pattern | Core Skill | Cadence | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sniff** | 2-10 sec | Intense, focused burst | Visual search | One and done | | **Decrypt** | 30-90 sec | Periodic check-ins | Pattern recognition / deduction | Process delay | | **Fuzz** | 15-45 sec | Rhythmic engagement (fire, read, adjust) | Spatial reasoning / estimation | Volley cycles | Fuzz sits neatly between the other two in both duration and attention demand. It's not as quick as Sniff, not as long as Decrypt. It doesn't demand constant eyes-on like Sniff, but it's more active between volleys than Decrypt. That's a good middle ground that gives you flexibility in how you deploy it across different signal types. The **probe → locate → exploit** arc also gives Fuzz the most satisfying *narrative shape* of the three. Sniff is utilitarian. Decrypt is cerebral. Fuzz *feels like hacking*. --- Want to explore any of these angles further, or are we ready to look at another system?
